speaker: SUB
text: You only use ten percent of your brain, right? I've heard that my whole life.

speaker: MAIN
text: fMRI scans show every region of your brain is active within a twenty-four hour period. Even during sleep, your brain uses twenty percent of your body's total energy. A ten-percent brain would use two percent. You'd be in a coma.

speaker: SUB
text: But where did this myth even come from?

speaker: MAIN
text: It's been traced to a misquote of William James from 1907. He said we only use a fraction of our mental potential — meaning we're lazy, not that our neurons are offline.

speaker: SUB
text: OK but I definitely feel like I'm not using my full brain sometimes.

speaker: MAIN
text: That feeling is real but the explanation is wrong. Your brain has eighty-six billion neurons. At any single moment, about one to sixteen percent fire simultaneously. If all fired at once? That's called a seizure.

speaker: SUB
text: So using a hundred percent of your brain is literally a medical emergency?

speaker: MAIN
text: Exactly. The brain works by selective activation. Different tasks light up different networks. A 2014 study in PNAS mapped over seventy thousand neural pathways and found every single one was used for something.

speaker: SUB
text: What about the movie Lucy? She unlocked a hundred percent and became superhuman.

speaker: MAIN
text: Hollywood loves this myth because it sells tickets. A 2013 survey by the Michael J. Fox Foundation found sixty-five percent of Americans still believe the ten-percent myth. Movies keep it alive.

speaker: SUB
text: Sixty-five percent? That's insane.

speaker: MAIN
text: It gets better. A separate study asked neuroscience students. Even among them, thirty-two percent believed it. A third of brain science students think most of their brain is just sitting there doing nothing.

speaker: SUB
text: OK so we use all of our brain. But can we make it work better?

speaker: MAIN
text: Here's the real twist. You can't unlock more brain, but you can optimize what you have. Neuroplasticity research shows learning new skills physically rewires neural connections. A London taxi driver study found their hippocampus was significantly larger than average.

speaker: SUB
text: So their brains literally grew from memorizing streets?

speaker: MAIN
text: Grew and restructured. Sleep also plays a massive role. A UC Berkeley study showed one night of sleep deprivation reduces hippocampal activity by forty percent. You don't need more brain — you need to stop sabotaging the one you have.

speaker: SUB
text: So what's the verdict?

speaker: MAIN
text: The ten-percent myth is completely false. You use your entire brain. The real issue isn't capacity — it's efficiency. Sleep, exercise, and learning new things are the only evidence-backed brain boosters.

speaker: SUB
text: No magic pills, no secret unlock codes.

speaker: MAIN
text: Just neuroscience.

speaker: SUB
text: Next week we're busting another myth — does cracking your knuckles actually cause arthritis?

speaker: MAIN
text: Subscribe so you don't miss it. We break one myth every week with actual science.
